coredata.dc:title: New voltammetric and spectroscopic studies to quinacrine-DNA-Cdots interaction
coredata.dc:creator: Oliveira, R.
Amaro, F.
Azevedo, M.
Vale, N.
Gonçalves, H.
Antunes, C.
Rego, R.
coredata.dc:description: The interaction of antimalarial drug quinacrine (QC) with salmon sperm double stranded DNA was studied on three sets of glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) modified with DNA, carbon dots (Cdots) and DNA + Cdots mixture at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4. For the first time, Cdots were used in studies of drug-DNA interactions. DNA binding was investigated by cyclic and differential pulse voltammetry (CV and DPV), fluorescence quenching, UV–vis absorption and FTIR spectroscopy, suggesting that QC acts as efficient DNA binder. DPV response of the QC on modified electrodes is pH-dependent. On modified GC electrodes the oxidation peak current/charge change and the oxidation peaks shifted to positive values. Stern-Volmer (KSV) and binding constants (Kf) determined from fluorescence measurements were KSV of 8.8 × 103 M−1 and Kf of 2.0 × 104 M−1 and KSV of 8.3 × 103 M−1 and Kf of 3.2 × 104 M−1, for pH 4.5 and 7.4, respectively. Static single binding intercalation seems to be the main interaction process between QC and DNA at both pH values. Moreover, UV–vis and FTIR studies confirmed the interaction between QC and DNA. The overall results suggested that QC binds to DNA essentially through intercalation mode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quinacrine-DNA interaction
Modified glassy carbon electrodes
C-dots
Differential pulse and cyclic voltammetry
Fluorescence
UV–vis and FTIR spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DNA, Cdots and DNA + Cdots modified GCEs preparation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the as-prepared Cdots: TEM images (a–c), DLS (d), XPS regions C 1s (e.1) and O 1s (e.2), FTIR spectrum (f) and 3D molecular structure (g).

Cyclic (a) and differential pulse (b) voltammograms of QC on GCE at pH values of 4.5 (blue line, concentration of 59.9 μM) and 7.4 (red line, concentration of 59.6 μM); scan rate: 40 mV s−1 (CV) and 5 mV s−1 (DPV). Cyclic and differential voltammograms were also recorded in pH 4.5 and 7.4 buffer solutions.

Cyclic (a) and differential pulse (b and c) voltammograms of QC on GCE-DNA with different DNA concentrations (CV/DPV, DNA1 0.196/0.160 mM; DNA2 19.6/15.9 mM) at pH values of 4.5 (a and b) and 7.4 (c); scan rate: 40 mV s−1 (CV) and 5 mV s−1 (DPV). For comparison purposes, CV and DPV curves of QC on GCE at pH values of 4.5 (blue line, concentration of 59.9 μM) and 7.4 (red line, concentration of 59.6 μM) are also shown.

Cyclic (a) and differential pulse (b and c) voltammograms of QC on GCE-Cdots (CV/DPV, 3.42 mg mL−1 Cdots) and GCE-DNA + Cdots (CV/DPV 0.392/0.317 mM DNA; CV/DPV 3.42 mg mL−1 Cdots (DNA 1:Cdots 1)) electrodes at pH values of 4.5 (a and b) and 7.4 (c). Scan rate: 40 mV s−1 (CV) and 5 mV s−1 (DPV). For comparison purposes, CV and DPV curves of QC on GCE at pH values of 4.5 (blue line, concentration of 59.9 μM) and 7.4 (red line, concentration of 59.6 μM) and GCE-DNA1 are also shown.

Fluorescence spectra of QC with increasing amounts of DNA to [DNA] ≈ 60 μM at pH values of 4.5 (a) and 7.4 (b); Arrows refer to fluorescence intensity percentage decrease at ca. 502 nm and λex = 440 nm.
Stern-Volmer quenching (F0/F vs [DNA]) and Scatchard (log [(F0 F)/F] vs log [DNA] plots for QC-DNA complexes at pH values of 4.5 (a and c) and 7.4 (b and d).

UV–vis absorption spectra of QC (59.9 μM), at pH values of 4.5 (a) and 7.4 (b), in the absence and presence of different concentrations of DNA (mM): (1) 0.069; (2) 0.136; (3) 0.200; (4) 0.261; (5) 0.321; (6) 0.378; (7) 0.433; (8) 0.487; (9) 0.538; (10) 0.588. Arrows indicate the absorbance intensity change upon increasing DNA concentration.
Linear plot of A0/(A-A0) vs 1/[DNA] at pH values of 4.5 (a) and 7.4 (b) acquired at 424 nm.
ATR-FTIR spectra of DNA and QC-DNA complex.

head.ce:title: New voltammetric and spectroscopic studies to quinacrine-DNA-Cdots interaction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interaction of antimalarial drug quinacrine (QC) with salmon sperm double stranded DNA was studied on three sets of glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) modified with DNA, carbon dots (Cdots) and DNA + Cdots mixture at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4. For the first time, Cdots were used in studies of drug-DNA interactions.

DNA binding was investigated by cyclic and differential pulse voltammetry (CV and DPV), fluorescence quenching, UV–vis absorption and FTIR spectroscopy, suggesting that QC acts as efficient DNA binder. DPV response of the QC on modified electrodes is pH-dependent. On modified GC electrodes the oxidation peak current/charge change and the oxidation peaks shifted to positive values. Stern-Volmer (KSV) and binding constants (Kf) determined from fluorescence measurements were KSV of 8.8 × 103 M−1 and Kf of 2.0 × 104 M−1 and KSV of 8.3 × 103 M−1 and Kf of 3.2 × 104 M−1, for pH 4.5 and 7.4, respectively. Static single binding intercalation seems to be the main interaction process between QC and DNA at both pH values. Moreover, UV–vis and FTIR studies confirmed the interaction between QC and DNA. The overall results suggested that QC binds to DNA essentially through intercalation mode.

ce:section.ce:para: Quinacrine (QC, Fig. 1 , IUPAC name of N 4-(6-chloro-2-methoxyacridin-9-yl)-N 1,N 1-diethylpentane-1,4-diamine) is a heterocyclic three-ring compound, and an acridine derivative (9-aminoacridine). This drug was the earliest effective antimalarial drug, but rising QC resistance made it difficult to control this debilitating disease, particularly in the most virulent of the human-infecting Plasmodium species, Plasmodium falciparum [1,2]. DNA is generally considered one of the biological targets for QC anticancer compounds. There are three general modes of binding that characterize the compound interactions with double stranded DNA: intercalation, groove binding and covalent binding [3–5]. Synthetic or natural QC drugs display varying chemical and biological properties but they share the common property of DNA intercalation. This is due to the presence of a QC “backbone” that confers a planar structure to the molecules, allowing them to intercalate into DNA by stacking between base pairs. The intercalation of several heterocyclic three-ring compounds has been demonstrated whereby the flat polyaromatic chromophore inserts between the base pairs of double-helical DNA. This process is driven by stacking and charge-transfer interactions between the aromatic systems of the acridine compounds and the DNA bases, resulting in unwinding of the helix [6,7]. The acridine derivative QC is no exception, it also binds to DNA by intercalation [8–10]. Fluorescence-assayed preferential binding studies of QC to DNA reveal that the neighbour base sequences influence the QC binding. In particular the sites where a GC base pair is involved were found to display high affinities [11]. The high affinity of QC for DNA via intercalation can be hampered by denaturation or depurination [9,12].

In addition to potential therapeutic applications, many small molecules capable of recognising DNA are postulated to be useful in electroanalytical applications [10]. Our goal was to develop new modified glassy carbon electrodes with carbon dots (Cdots) and DNA to study the DNA binding to QC.

Cdots are carbon-based nanoparticles that have been gathering much attention, particularly for bioimaging, drug delivery, sensing and theranostics development [13,14]. Cdots nanomaterials display remarkable physical-chemical properties including: (1) optical properties, (2) excellent biocompatibility, (3) very low cytotoxicity, (4) tuneable surface functionalities, (5) the abundant and inexpensive nature [14]. One of their most appealing features is the fact that they can be successfully produced from waste biomass using scalable procedures [13].

Although these nanoparticles are quite versatile and can act as electrochemical sensors/platforms for several species, mainly due to the surface groups reactivity [15,16], recognition elements are needed to combine with Cdots in order to develop sensitive and specific sensing probes [17,18]. This is the case of DNA, a biological target for anticancer compounds. The interaction drug-DNA can cause chemical and conformational modifications. Then, electrochemistry coupled with spectroscopy techniques may provide a relatively easy way to obtain useful insights into the molecular mechanism of drug-DNA interaction, which could be an important step in the development of a new drug [19].

Here we prepared an ink of Cdots and DNA and dropped it onto the electrode in order to test its sensibility in measuring QC-DNA binding by differential pulse (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV). These results were further validate with fluorescence quenching, UV–vis absorption and FTIR experiments.
Quinacrine dihydrochloride (Q3251, Sigma-Aldrich), salmon sperm double stranded DNA sodium salt (D1626, Sigma-Aldrich) and other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

Quinacrine (QC) solution of about 60 μM were prepared by dissolving required amounts of solid quinacrine dihydrochloride in 0.1 M acetate (pH 4.5) and phosphate (pH 7.4) buffer solutions and stored at 4 °C.
High-purity Milli-Q water (resistivity 18 MΩ cm, Interlab Sistem Purist UV set) was used in all experiments.

Dilute solutions of DNA were prepared from a stock solution of ca. 6 mg mL−1 in water. The solutions were kept at least 1 h to achieve the complete DNA solubility before being used and/or to prepare new DNA aqueous solutions. The DNA concentration (in moles of base pairs per liter) was determined by UV–vis absorbance at 260 nm considering the molar absorptivity of 6600 M−1 cm−1 [20]. Both solutions were stored at 2–4 °C prior to using. Purity of DNA solutions was checked by monitoring the ratio of its absorbance at 260 nm to that at 280 nm, giving values of ca. 1.8, characteristic of a protein-free DNA.

The Cdots synthesis was performed by preparing a concentrated solution of glucose in water (50 mL) until it forms a clear solution (1.0 M). H2SO4 (50 mL, 95–97%, Sigma-Aldrich) was added and the mixed solution was ultrasonically treated for 4 h, and oven-dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The black resulting solution was mixed with water (50 mL), centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 1 h, and filtered to remove the solid supernatant. A dark brown solution was obtained along with the formation of the Cdots. Afterwards nanoparticles were neutralized with NaOH (10 M) and the salts formed were removed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 1 h. The obtained supernatant was further centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min and loaded into dialysis molecular membrane (molecular mass cut-off: 500–1000 Da) and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 48 h. The Cdots suspension was then lyophilized for 48 h and a brown powder was obtained and used without further treatment.

To perform the Cdots dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis a diluted solution of Cdots was passed through two continuous 200 nm Fischer Scientific RC filters in order to assure that any measurement above this value was due to nanoparticle aggregation and was not representative of the nanoparticles size.

The DLS analysis was performed using a Malvern Instruments (Malvern, UK) Zeta Sizer Nano ZS, and disposable polystyrene cells from Sigma. The X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Kratos AXIS Ultra HSA, with VISION software for data acquisition and CasaXPS software for data analysis. The analysis was carried out with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.7 eV), operating at 15 kV (90 W), in fixed analyzer transmission (FAT) mode, with a pass energy of 40 eV for regions ROI and 80 eV for survey. Data acquisition was performed with a pressure lower than 1.0 × 10−6 Pa, and it was used a charge neutralization system. The effect of the electric charge was corrected by the reference of the carbon peak (285 eV). The deconvolution of spectra may be carried out using the XPSPEAK41 or CasaXPS programs, in which a peak fitting is performed using Gaussian-Lorentzian peak shape and Shirley type background subtraction. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the Cdots was carried out on a Jeol JEM 1400, Zeiss model EM 10C with a SC1000 Orius TM CCD camera gatan using TEM copper-based grids.

For spectroscopic studies, titration experiments were performed using a fixed amount of QC (ca. 60 μM) with incremental addition of DNA solution. Fluorescence emission and UV–vis spectra were recorded for QC solution in absence and in presence of DNA. The titration procedure was done in triplicate for each pH under study.

Fluorescence emission measurements were made on Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette of 1 cm. The slits widths for excitation and emission were kept at 5 nm for QC pH 4.5 solution, while for QC pH 7.4 solution the slit for emission was kept at 2.5 nm. Emission spectra were recorded, at room temperature, in the 440–700 nm range using an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The spectra smoothing process was done in the OriginPro 8 v8.0724 software with the Savitzky-Golay method considering 20 points of windows and a second order polynomial.
UV–vis measurements were performed in a Perkin Elmer double beam Lambda 25 UV/VIS Spectrometer. The absorbance spectra were recorded in the wavelength 200–700 nm range.

FTIR spectroscopy measurements were performed in IRAffinity-1S Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrophotometer Shimadzu with an ATR module and the LabSolutions IR 2.13 software. Absorbance spectra were recorded in the wavenumber 400-4000 cm−1 range with 4 cm−1 resolution and average of 126 scans. Two aqueous solutions were prepared: 2.5 mM QC and a 6.01 mg mL−1 DNA. A third solution was obtained by mixing 2 mL of QC solution with 1 mL of DNA solution. These three samples were left to evaporate at ambient conditions, obtaining homogeneous residues, which were used for the FTIR measurements.

Electrochemical analyses were carried out at room temperature using a typical three-electrode-cell setup and a PGSTAT302 Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat instrument controlled by the software Autolab, Nova 1.11. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (geometric area 0.196 cm2, Metrohm RDE.GC50 80392), a Pt foil and a double junction Ag/AgCl, 3.5 M KCl (Hanna Instruments, HI-5311) were used as working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. All the potentials given in this work are referred to the latter electrode.

Before electrochemical experiments, GCE was polished with alumina cleaning kit (Metrohm 6.2802.000), washed with Milli-Q water (often ultrasonically cleaned in Milli-Q water for 5 min), and finally dried in air.

CV and DPV were performed in N2-saturated electrolyte solutions and during the tests a mild N2 flow was kept above the electrolyte. The CV tests were carried out in the potential range of −0.2–1.2 V at 40 mV s−1 scan rate. DPV curves were obtained in the potential range from 0.4 to 1.4 V, with equilibration time 10 s, pulse amplitude 50 mV, pulse width 70 ms, step potential 1 mV, interval time 0.2 s and scan rate 5 mV s−1 [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA, Cdots and DNA + Cdots modified GCEs preparation
ce:section.ce:para: To study the interaction QC-DNA three sets of modified electrodes were prepared: (1) GCE-DNA, (2) GCE-Cdots and (3) GCE-DNA + Cdots.

The Cdots ink was prepared by ultrasonically mixing 3.3 mg of Cdots in 0.9645 mL of water for 30 min. Before each experiment, the as-prepared suspension was placed again in the ultrasonic bath for 10 min.
Then, 50 μL of the Cdots dispersion was added to 50 μL of DNA solution and the resulting mixture was carefully mixed for few seconds and kept cold. A well-distributed dispersion was then obtained.

Next, 5 μL of the Cdots ink (GCE-Cdots), or DNA (GCE-DNA) or mixtures of DNA and Cdots (1:1) solution (GCE-DNA + Cdots) were dropped onto the surface of GCE and dried in air for 30 min at room temperature.
Finally, the modified electrodes were immersed into QC solution at both pH values of 4.5 and 7.4 during 5 min before CV and DPV were recorded.
The electrochemical characterization was repeated at least three times in order to confirm the reproducibility of the results.

The raw Cdots were produced via acid dehydration of carbohydrates. This synthetic method consists in the degradation of carbohydrates in an acidic media in order to obtain small homogeneous sized C-based nanoparticles. In this process the carbohydrate is decomposed through a cascade of chemical reactions until small C clusters start to form and nucleation occurs [22]. The Cdots size is reaction time dependent and the surface groups and thermal stability are strongly related to the starting carbohydrate composition. Since these characteristics are important to their application it was necessary to fully study them and different techniques were used, such as, DLS, TEM, XPS, FTIR (Fig. 2 ).

There are two major size populations at average values of 49 and 232 nm. The appearance of a 232 nm population led us to conclude that there was particle aggregation present even though the solution was previously passed through 200 nm filters and sonicated immediately before the DLS measurement (Fig. 2d). This was further confirmed by the TEM analysis that clearly shows small nanoparticles aggregated. Indeed, the electric repulsion of the surface charge is not enough to prevent self-aggregation and even though smaller particles can be found, in typical water dispersions, is it more likely to find aggregates of 49 nm than individual nanoparticles (Fig. 2a–c).

The XPS survey spectra show clear and distinct peaks of carbon (C 1s, 285.0 eV) and oxygen (O 1s, 531.9 eV) (Fig. 2e-1 and e-2, respectively). XPS results are in good accordance with FTIR analysis (Fig. 2f), where it is possible to see the vibrational modes of OH (3409 cm−1) typical in carboxylic acids and OH (2949 cm−1) characteristic of alcohols, CCO (2140 cm−1), CC (2089 cm−1), CO (1704 cm−1), CO (1250 cm−1), COC (1178 cm−1), CO, CH (1400 cm−1) typical of carboxilic acids and CH bending (617 cm−1). These results are in agreement with the one reported by Nie et al. [23].

During the nucleation process and the subsequent Cdots growth there are various possible structural configurations using the same functional groups, which leads to different surface states; therefore, it is not possible to say the percentage of each functional group in each nanoparticle, nonetheless these functional groups are present at their surface and can easily react with other molecules that they become in contact with. C nanoparticles and nanotubes are hydrophobic; however, the characteristics of the surface groups present in the synthetized Cdots make them highly water soluble which is a major advantage when working with DNA sensing systems (Fig. 2g). In addition, Cdots showed good thermal stability (results not shown).

Representative CV and DPV curves of the QC solutions (concentration ca. 60 μM) at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4 are shown in Fig. 3 . It is worth to note that the CV curves exhibit broader and more undefined peaks than those corresponding to DPV curves. DPV (pulsed technique) is highly sensitive because the charging current (nonfaradaic process) is minimized while the faradaic current is increased. In CV, both faradaic and nonfaradaic processes take place [24].

At GCE, and for both CV and DPV curves the pH of the solution influenced on the peak potential and peak current/charge of QC, suggesting the participation of protons in the redox process [25]. The peak potentials shifted towards more negative potentials with increasing pH.

Buffer solutions did not exhibit any redox behaviour, whereas steady CV curves of QC showed redox peaks at a scan rate of 40 mV s−1 (Fig. 3a). At pH 4.5, and in the positive potential scan, the highest oxidation peak was located at ca. 0.812 V (peak current 16.3 μA), and it was followed by 3 smaller peaks at ca. 0.906, 0.094 and 0.285 V (Fig. 3a). On the cathodic sweep it was possible to observe 2 peaks at 0.047 and 0.248 V. At pH 7.4 QC exhibited an anodic multi-component peak at ca. 0.681 V (peak current 8.85 μA) and a shoulder peak at about 1.04 V. The current response for pH 4.5 is higher than that of pH 7.4 and the reduction peaks are not observed at pH 7.4. The anodic voltammetric behaviour of QC obtained in this work at both pH are to some extent similar to that of acridine in different solutions [26].

At both pH, the linear dependence of the peak current vs the square root of the scan rate in range 10–200 mV s−1 was found, suggesting a diffusion-controlled redox process of QC on GCE (results not shown).

For optimized DPV curves and at 5 mV s−1 scan rate, QC exhibited well-defined peaks at 0.725 V (peak current/charge 9.63 μA/8.98 μC) and 0.622 V (peak current/charge 4.99 μA/7.42 μC) at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, respectively (Fig. 3b and Table 1 ). In this work the QC oxidation peak current at pH 7.4 (4.99 μA) is ca. 6 times higher than that obtained by using square wave voltammetry (0.71 V, ca. 0.850 μA, 90 μM QC, pH 7.0) [10].
In this study, a DNA polymer modified GCE was used to study the interaction of QC with DNA alloying or blocking the binding of drug molecules to the electrode surface [27].
Fig. 4 shows the CV and DPV curves of QC (ca. 60 μM) on DNA modified GCE. For comparison purposes, the CV and DPV curves of QC on GCE are also shown.

With the increase in DNA concentration onto GCE (DNA1 0.196 mM; DNA2 19.6 mM), the CV curves of QC at pH 4.5 changed as is evidenced by the sequential drop in peak current and the peak potential shift in the positive direction mainly for DNA1 (Fig. 4a).

DPV curves of QC at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4 are shown in Fig. 4b and c and are pH-dependent. At pH 4.5 the QC oxidation peaks showed a gradual decrease in peak current with increasing DNA concentrations (DNA1 0.160 mM; DNA2 15.9 mM) onto the GCE (Table 1). This trend in the peak current was similar to that obtained for CV tests. This suggest the partial blockage of QC diffusion by DNA film.

On the contrary, for DPV curves at pH 7.4 the trend is different, being the oxidation current peak/charge of QC with DNA1 higher than that of QC on GCE (Table 1). A positive shift in the peak potential of QC oxidation peak was observed for both DNA concentrations and at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4. The diluted DNA solutions will be used in subsequent experiments.

In recent years, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [28], multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) [29–31], single-walled CNT (SWCNT)/polymer modified pencil graphite electrode [32], pencil graphite electrode [33], graphene [34], reduced graphene oxide [31], screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) [35,36], carbon black [37] and so on, have been used on the interaction studies of drugs with DNA and in the field of electrochemical DNA biosensors.

Cdots were used to improve electron-transfer rate, to increase the electroactive surface area of GCE, to enhance strength of the signal and to interact with DNA [14,15]. Therefore, carbon dots may represent a promising electrochemical probe to study the interaction between a drug and DNA.
To the best of our knowledge there is no data in the literature about the interaction binding between QC and DNA onto DNA + Cdots modified GC electrodes.

The cyclic voltammetric response of the QC on modified GCEs at pH 4.5 changed as is observed by the marked decrease of the main oxidation peak in the order GCE > GCE-DNA1 > GCE-DNA + Cdots 1:1 > GCE-Cdots, while the peaks shifted to positive values, except for GCE-Cdots (Fig. 5 a).
The CV and DPV results on GCE-DNA1 are also shown because the DNA concentrations on this modified electrode (CV/DPV 0.196/0.160 mM) are similar than those of GCE-DNA + Cdots (CV/DPV 0.196/0.159 mM).

Fig. 5b and c shows the comparison of the results of electrochemical behaviour of QC on GCE-Cdots and GCE-DNA + Cdots 1:1 by DPV at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, being pH-dependent. For comparison purposes, DPV curves of QC on GCE and GCE-DNA1 are also shown.

At pH 4.5 QC is oxidized at both electrodes, but the peak current/charge on GCE (0.752 V, peak current/charge 9.63 μA/8.98 μC) is larger than those on GCE-Cdots and GCE-DNA + Cdots. Interestingly, the peak current/charge of QC on both electrodes (6.02 μA/6.13 μC and 5.73 μA/6.58 μC on GCE-Cdots and GCE-DNA + Cdots, respectively) is comparable (Fig. 5b and Table 1) and it is not fully understood at the present time and requires additional studies. The decrease in peak current and peak potential shift in the positive direction of QC on modified GCE-DNA + Cdots 1:1 suggest the intercalation binding of QC to the large slowly diffusing DNA molecule [38]. Our results are in agreement with those previously presented for example for gatifloxacin, sulfadiazine and nitazoxanide at a pH 4.0 to 6.6 range [29,30,36].

At pH 7.4 the peak oxidation current of QC on both electrodes give rise to almost similar current responses GCE-Cdots (6.00 μA) and GCE-DNA + Cdots (6.33 μA) (Fig. 5c and Table 1), but higher than those obtained on GCE (4.99 μA). Moreover, the peak potential shifted positively, except on GCE-Cdots. This is an indication of QC binding with DNA on the electrode surface [28,35,36].

QC can form four protonated states under varying pH conditions (QcH, QcH2 +, QcH3 2+ and QcH4 3+) [39] and it is anticipated that the QC-DNA complex depends on QC protonated states and is pH-dependent. Work is underway to gain more fundamental understanding of the electrochemical behaviour of QC (different concentrations) or DNA (different DNA + Cdots suspension ratio) for sensing with GCE-Cdots and/or GCE-DNA + Cdots. However, this has not been intended in this work.
To further confirm the interaction between QC and DNA, fluorescence, UV–vis absorption and FTIR experiments were carried out.
QC shows fluorescence at room temperature in solution in absence or in the presence of DNA, and their binding can be directly predicted through changes in emission spectra.

The fluorescence emission spectra of QC (concentration about 60 μM) with increasing DNA concentrations to 50–60 μM are shown in Fig. 6 a and b, at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, respectively. At both pH values, the intensity of the fluorescence spectra emission band of the QC at a maximum wavelength of about 502 nm was gradually quenched upon DNA addition (Fig. 6). Emission-quenching phenomena reflects the interaction of QC with DNA and maybe due to an effective electron transfer between an excited state of drug molecule and DNA bases [40].

The acridine derivative QC is a well-known DNA intercalating drug [6,41,42]. In the case of intercalating drugs, the molecules are inserted into the base stack of the helix. The rotation of the free molecules prefers the radiationless deactivation of the excited states but if the drugs are bound to DNA the deactivation through fluorescence emission is favored and a significant increase in fluorescence emission is usually observed [38,43]. However, fluorescence binding studies of QC to DNA revealed that the neighbour base sequences influence the binding of QC. Fluorescence quenching is mainly observed in guanine (G)-cytosine (C) rich polynucleotides [44]. The salmon species used in this work is Oncerhynchus keta with a G-C content of 41.2%. The change in fluorescence intensity of QC by DNA addition at 502 nm was 37 and 32% for pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, respectively (Fig. 6a and b). For instance, E. coli with a 50% G-C content, the fluorescence intensity change of QC mustard, at 514 nm is 32.4% [45]. Our results are in agreement with the quenching effect reported by other authors [11,41,42,45,46].

Since ionic strength was high in all experiments due to high Na+ concentration of the buffer solutions together with low QC concentration, QC-DNA external electrostatic binding can be negligible and intercalation may be considered the dominant interaction mode [46].

Fluorescence quenching data was analyzed with Stern-Volmer equation given by eq. (1) [38,47]:
(1)

F
= 1 +

K
SV
[ DNA ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of QC fluorophore in the absence and presence of different concentrations of quencher (DNA), respectively. [DNA] is the concentration of DNA and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The bimolecular quenching rate constant is k q and the lifetime of fluorophore (τ 0) in the absence of DNA is 10−8 s [38,47]. The quenching process efficiency was deduced from the plot of F0/F vs the DNA concentration and KSV (a measure of the quenching process) is obtained from the slope of the straight lines (Fig. 7 a and b). The Stern-Volmer plots are linear, suggesting a single quenching mechanism in experimental conditions used [47] (Fig. 7a and b).

The values of KSV 8.8 × 103 ± 0.2 and 8.3 × 103 ± 0.2 M-1, respectively, for pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, indicate that the QC is bound to DNA. k q is higher than the limiting diffusion rate constant for a biomacromolecule (2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1), indicating the prevalence of a static quenching mechanism [38,47]. For comparison, KSV value of QC-Hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) complex (4:1 w/v; 0.25 mM:0.07 mM) is 5.0 × 103 M−1. This complex is important to evaluate QC as a potential amyloid imaging compound because amyloid plaque is associated with several neuronal and non-neuronal degenerative diseases [48].

To determine the strength of the interaction of complexes with DNA, the values of the binding constant (Kf) and number of binding sites (n) were determined from the Scatchard equation given by eq. (2) [38,49]:
(2)
log
(
F
0
−
F
F
)
= log
K
f
+ n
log
[
DNA
]

The plots of
log
(
F
0
− F
F
)
vs log [DNA] concentration are shown in Fig. 7c and d, at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, respectively. The values for Kf were found to be 2.0 × 104 M−1 and 3.2 × 104 M−1, and n = 1.11 and n = 1.15, at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, respectively. The high values of Kf clearly highlights the high affinity of QC to DNA at both pH [38]. n was approximately unity, indicating that a single binding site in QC molecule preferentially binds with DNA [49] or QC intercalates into DNA at approximately every base pair at both pH values of 4.5 and 7.4 [10]. The values of Kf obtained in this work are lower to that reported for QC using voltammetry (1.59 × 105 M−1) [10] and are similar/higher to those obtained by fluorescence (range 3.55–0.9 × 104 M−1) for different DNAs [45]. For acridine-thiosemicarbazone derivatives-DNA complexes the values of Kf obtained from UV–vis ranged from 104 to 106 M−1, that is typical for intercalation dyes-DNA complexes [40].

In order to corroborate the results obtained by fluorescence data the interaction of QC with DNA was also studied by spectrophotometric titration. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy can provide important information regarding the mode and strength binding of drugs to DNA through the monitoring modifications in the spectra during the titration procedure. Indeed, the interaction between drugs and DNA can cause variation on spectral absorption properties such as, red/blue shift, hyperchromic/hypochromic effects and isochromatic point [50]. Based on this variation, qualitative and quantitative information about drug-DNA interaction can be obtained.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of QC in the absence and the presence of different DNA concentrations at pH values of 4.5 and 7.4, are presented in Fig. 8 a and b, respectively. In the absence of DNA the spectra of QC, at both pH, are similar and, as can be seen, presented an intense peak at 275 nm and a broad peak in the visible zone (380–500 nm). The results shown that the changes observed in the absorption spectra of QC in the presence of increasing amount of DNA are very similar at both pH under study. With the addition of DNA the intensity at 270 nm increased (hyperchromic) and a shift to shorter wavelength (blue shift) was observed. In contrast, the intensity at 424 nm decrease (hypochromic) and a red shift was shown. These changes identified in the QC spectra in the presence of DNA, hypochromic effect and red shift, suggest intercalation as a mechanism of interaction between QC and DNA. To investigate the intensity of the interaction between QC and DNA, the binding constant (Kf) was calculated according to the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (3) [50]:
(3)
A
0
A −
A
0
=
ε
QC
ε
QC − DNA
−
ε
QC
+
(
ε
QC
ε
QC − DNA
−
ε
QC
)
(
1
K
f
[
DNA
]
)
where A0 and A are the absorbances of QC at 424 nm in the absence and presence of DNA, respectively, and ɛQC and ɛQC-DNA are their molar absorption coefficients. The binding constant was calculated by the ratio of the slope to intercept of the plot A0/(A-A0) vs 1/[DNA], shown in Fig. 9 . In this study, Kf with values of 661 and 598 M−1 were obtained for QC-DNA complex at pH 4.5 and 7.4, respectively. The Kf values are lower than those obtained from fluorescence data, which can be due to the difference between the methods used and DNA concentration range used in both studies.

The main infrared FTIR spectral features of DNA and the influence of QC addition are shown in Fig. 10 . In this study, the region under analysis is confined to the 1800-700 cm−1 range, since it contains the main information under analysis in this work, namely ring vibrations of nitrogenous bases (CO, CN stretching), PO2 stretching vibration and deoxyribose stretching of the DNA backbone. The bands in the DNA spectrum at 1710, 1650, 1601 and 1485 cm−1 are assigned to guanine, thymine, adenine and cytosine, respectively. In addition, the phosphate groups of DNA exhibit anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations bands at 1219 e 1087 cm−1, respectively [51–53]. These are the major bands of pure DNA which are monitored during the current study. When DNA is in QC presence, changes in these bands are observed as shown in Fig. 10.

QC-DNA spectrum shown several changes, but the appearance of a new absorption band at 1170 cm−1 is particularly significant. Additionally, it is also possible to see a slight shift (2 cm−1) towards lower wavenumbers of the band previously centered around 1219 cm−1. In relation to other DNA bands, cytosine band shifts (2 cm−1) towards higher wavenumbers, while thymine and guanine bands shift (3 cm−1) towards lower wavenumbers. Also, the absorption band of adenine completely disappeared after interaction with QC. These shifting with minor intensity variation are indicative of intercalative mode binding of QC with DNA [51]. Intercalation occurs through hydrogen bonding with nitrogen base pairs. All these variations in spectral features indicate that QC interact with nitrogenous bases, namely with adenine, as well as, with phosphate group of DNA backbone [27,54]. In conclusion, the FTIR results indicate that in the presence of QC the main characteristics bands of DNA nitrogenous bases undergo changes, corroborating the interaction between QC and DNA.

Three sets of modified electrodes were prepared: GCE-DNA, GCE-Cdots and GCE-DNA + Cdots. The binding of QC with DNA resulted in a series of changes in electrochemical behaviour and spectral characteristics.

DPV curves showed that QC is oxidized in all the modified electrodes and the oxidation is pH-dependent. At pH 4.5 the peak current/charge on GCE-DNA + Cdots electrodes were lower than those on GCE, while, in contrast, at pH 7.4 an increase of peak current/charge is observed. A positive shift in peak potential of QC oxidation was observed for both pH values 4.5 and 7.4. This behaviour is similar to that obtained for CV curves at pH 4.5. The current peak/charge variation and peak potential shift in the positive direction suggest the QC binding with DNA on the electrode surface.

Fluorescence, UV–vis and FTIR analysis further confirmed the electrochemical results. Fluorescence quenching results suggest that, at pH values 4.5 and 7.4, QC mainly undergoes a static single binding intercalation process with DNA. UV–vis studies revealed a hypochromic and bathochromic nature of QC-DNA interaction. FTIR analysis of DNA before and after binding showed involvement of nitrogen bases and phosphate groups of DNA.

Cdots may be a promising alternative to GC electrodes, to study drug-DNA interaction, and for QC or DNA sensing. Work is underway to gain more understanding of the behaviour of QC and different drugs on GCE-DNA + Cdots electrodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by FEDER, through COMPETE and Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (FCT) (UID/QUI/00616/2013, project LUMECD (ref. POCI-01-0145-FEDER-016884 and PTDC/CTM-NAN/0956/2014) and project UniRCell (ref. SAICTPAC/0032/2015, POCI-01-0145-FEDER-016422). R. Oliveira thanks FCT for UniRCell grant (BIM/UTAD/43/2017). NV acknowledges support from FCT and FEDER (European Union), award number IF/00092/2014/CP1255/CT0004. NV also thanks FCT for the IF position.
